retrieved_docs.title: Differentiating Delirium Versus Dementia in Older Adults -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium Infections : Urinary tract infections, pneumonia, sepsis, meningitis, encephalitis Metabolic disturbances : Hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, hyponatremia, hypercalcemia, hypoxia, hepatic or renal failure Medications/toxins : Anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids, polypharmacy, alcohol withdrawal CNS disorders : Stroke, traumatic brain injury, epilepsy/postictal state, increased intracranial pressure Systemic illnesses : Decompensated heart failure, malignancies, dehydration, thyroid dysfunction Psychiatric conditions : Acute psychosis, severe depression, mania [5] [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: Other drugs. Although rivastigmine, donepezil, and physostigmine have been proposed for ICU delirium management, evidence of effectiveness is currently very scarce.

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: In the general medical ward, there is some evidence that targeting modifiable risk factors and multicomponent patient-centered approaches may decrease the incidence and average duration of delirium. These include interventions improving cognitive impairment, good sleep hygiene, mobility, vision, and hearing. Besides, strategies for preventing infection, dehydration, constipation, and hypoxia are mandatory. [32] Currently, no pharmacological agents have enough evidence to recommend their use in preventing delirium. In the ICU setting, mechanically ventilated adult patients at risk of developing delirium may benefit from dexmedetomidine infusions (e.g., 0.1 μg/kg per hour) over BDZs infusions in regards to decreasing the prevalence of delirium. [33] Melatonin is probably useful for ICU delirium, but further studies are needed. [34] There is also evidence that early mobilization of the adult ICU patient population may reduce the duration and incidence of delirium.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: While prevention and nonpharmacologic interventions are the mainstays of treatment for delirium, it may occasionally be necessary to utilize pharmacologic therapies, which are only considered appropriate in limited circumstances. Patients suffering from delirium due to substance withdrawal may need the appropriate pharmacologic treatments, for example, using benzodiazepines to treat alcohol withdrawal. Delirium at the end of life can require pharmacologic therapies to alleviate the patient's pain and suffering. There are no recommended pharmacologic treatments for hypoactive delirium. A patient with hyperactive delirium whose behavior is a threat to themselves or others may need pharmacologic treatment. Treating the underlying cause of delirium with necessary medications, such as antibiotics, in the instance of infection is always appropriate. In the case of hyperactive delirium with behaviors presenting a risk to the patient or others, antipsychotics are the recommended first-line treatment if not contraindicated due to another comorbidity. Frequently used options include haloperidol, quetiapine, and risperidone. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Geriatric Evaluation and Treatment of Age-Related Cognitive Decline -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Aging changes processing speed, sensory perception, concept formation, abstraction, and mental flexibility. A comprehensive CGA enables healthcare professionals to distinguish between normal cognitive decline associated with aging and common conditions that can mimic or contribute to cognitive decline. The common conditions that may add to cognitive decline include: Infections of the urinary tract, pneumonia, sepsis, and abscesses Vitamin B12 and folic acid deficiencies Hypo- or hyperthyroidism Hypo- or hyperglycemia Hypoxemia Hypo- or hypernatremia Delirium Depression Pathological grief reaction Medications such as benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, and antihypertensives MCI Alzheimer disease Pick disease Lewy body disease Vascular and multi-infarct dementia Frontotemporal dementia Parkinson dementia Alcohol-related dementia Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Etiology -- Risk Factors for Delirium
retrieved_docs.content: The literature has identified multiple risk factors. Among these, the most studied are mentioned: [8] Older age Cognitive impairment Visual impairment Alcohol abuse Respiratory disorder Illness severity Terminal illnesses Comorbidity Infection Major surgery (e.g., complex abdominal, hip fracture, and cardiac surgery) Interestingly, a mnemonic bundle (DELIRIUM) was proposed (Saint Louis University Geriatrics Division and St. Louis Veterans Affairs) for assessing the main causes of delirium:

retrieved_docs.title: Agitation -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management of Severe Agitation
retrieved_docs.content: The Society of Critical Care Medicine (SCCM) 2018 Practice Guidelines suggest not routinely using haloperidol to treat delirium, as the evidence indicates that the use of haloperidol or an atypical antipsychotic medication is not associated with a shorter duration of delirium, mechanical ventilation, ICU length of stay, or decreased mortality. Despite the lack of evidence, short-term use of haloperidol or an atypical antipsychotic medication may be considered for agitated patients who may physically harm themselves or others. The SCCM conditionally recommends using dexmedetomidine for delirium in mechanically ventilated adults where agitation is precluding weaning/extubation. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: Dexmedetomidine. This alpha-2-adrenergic agonist with sedative, analgesic, and anxiolytic actions is useful in adults under MV when hyperactive delirium can obstacle the weaning. Due to the low-quality evidence of investigations assessed, a panel of experts did not recommend its use. [27] Side effects include bradycardia and hypotension or hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: While prevention and nonpharmacologic interventions are the mainstays of treatment for delirium, it may occasionally be necessary to utilize pharmacologic treatments, which are only considered appropriate in limited circumstances. Patients suffering from delirium due to substance withdrawal may need the appropriate pharmacologic treatments, for example, using benzodiazepines to treat alcohol withdrawal. Delirium at the end of life can require pharmacologic treatments to alleviate the patient's pain and suffering at the end of life. There are no recommended pharmacologic treatments for hypoactive delirium. A patient with hyperactive delirium whose behavior is a threat to themselves or others may need pharmacologic treatment. It is always appropriate to treat the underlying cause of delirium with necessary medications, such as antibiotics, in the instance of infection. In the case of hyperactive delirium with behaviors presenting a risk to the patient or others, antipsychotics are the recommended first-line treatment if not contraindicated due to another comorbidity. Frequently used options include haloperidol, quetiapine, and risperidone. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: There is much debate regarding the utility of antipsychotics and other medications such as physostigmine, rivastigmine, and donepezil in preventing and decreasing the duration of delirium. In the ICU setting, although there is no evidence that treatment with haloperidol will reduce the duration of delirium, it is the most commonly adopted treatment. Moreover, there is some evidence that atypical antipsychotics may be useful.

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Etiology -- Risk Factors for Delirium
retrieved_docs.content: D=DrugsE=Eyes, ears, and other sensory deficitsL=Low O2 states such as heart attack, stroke, and pulmonary embolismI=InfectionR=Retention (of urine or stool)I=Ictal stateU=Underhydraton/undernutritionM=Metabolic causes (e.g., diabetes, sodium abnormalities) Within the ICU setting, numerous risks have been identified: [9] Pre-existing dementia Mechanical ventilation [10] Sepsis History of hypertension High severity of illness on admission Pain Stroke Psychiatric disorders Depression Traumatic head injury Myocardial infarction COPD Steroids [11] Hypertension [12] Psychoactive medications including narcotics [13] Deep levels of sedation Environmental factors such as the absence of visible sunlight [13] Immobility and physical restraints Poor sleep quality Social factors like alcohol abuse and smoking [13] Lack of visitors [13] Anticholinergic drug exposure (e.g., diphenhydramine, promethazine, and cyclobenzaprine) [14]

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: It is generally accepted that there are no identified therapies (medications or interventions) proven to decrease the duration of delirium. As a consequence, treating the underlying physiological insult is of key importance. Also, other interventions should be considered for delirium management: Treatment of the underlying cause Correction of potential electrolyte disturbances Removal of offending pharmacological agents Maintain proper sleep/wake cycles Manage pain Address sensory impairments (hearing, vision) Encourage family visits and frequent reorientation. Early mobilization Interventions for symptomatic ICU treatment can be divided into pharmacological and non-pharmacological strategies. Medications

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium is a clinical syndrome that usually develops in the elderly. It is characterized by an alteration of attention, consciousness, and cognition, with a reduced ability to focus, sustain or shift attention. It develops over a short period and fluctuates during the day. The clinical presentation can vary, usually with psychomotor behavioral disturbances such as hyperactivity or hypoactivity and impairment in sleep duration and architecture. By definition, delirium is caused by an underlying medical condition and is not better explained by another preexisting, evolving, or established neurocognitive disorder. The underlying cause of delirium can vary widely and involve anything that stresses the baseline homeostasis of a vulnerable patient. Examples include substance intoxication or withdrawal, medication side effects, infection, surgery, metabolic derangements, pain, or even simple conditions such as constipation or urinary retention. The diagnosis is often missed, especially the hypoactive type, due to its subtle clinical manifestation. Delirium is dangerous, often preventable, and associated with a significant cost burden and increased morbidity and mortality. Efforts should focus on prevention, early diagnosis, and treatment of the underlying cause. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of delirium and the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: Short-acting benzodiazepines (BDZs). These agents, such as midazolam and lorazepam, can have a neurogenic effect. They are exclusively useful in patients with alcohol or sedative withdrawal or for delirium resulting from seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: ICU Delirium -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological and Non-pharmacological Management Strategies:
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics. This category includes haloperidol and atypical antipsychotics. Haloperidol is a dopamine (D2) receptor antagonist. It is given at the dosage of 2-10 mg (IV every six h); it is useful, especially in the hyperactive form. Atypical antipsychotics used for this purpose are olanzapine (IM 5-10 mg; max: 30 mg/d), risperidone (0.5-8 mg), quetiapine (orally 50 mg; max 400 mg/d), and ziprasidone. A special issue concerns antipsychotic-related toxicity. For instance, cardiotoxicity such as QT interval (QT) prolongation, torsade de pointes, and hypotension are reported at high doses of haloperidol, whereas a dosage of 2 mg can be safely administered. [24] Of note, caution should be taken when antipsychotics are used with other QT-prolonging medications. Other commonly used classes of drugs that are associated with QT prolongation include, but are not limited to, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, antiemetics, and antidepressants, as well as methadone, lithium, octreotide, and tacrolimus, among others. A baseline EKG and further assessment may be warranted. Antipsychotics can also induce extrapyramidal symptoms (EPSs) expressed as parkinsonism featuring dystonic reactions or akathisia. [25] Rarely, haloperidol can cause neuroleptic malignant syndrome [26] , whereas insomnia and agitation can often be observed. In the case of atypical antipsychotics, EPSs can manifest at high doses. Again, olanzapine and quetiapine may induce excessive sedation, whereas ziprasidone is more associated with QT prolongation.

The non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (SMQ) similarly features apathy (اللامبالاة), attention impairment, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Consequently, excessive daytime sleepiness serves as a linking symptom across arousal disturbances in both encephalopathic and delirium‑like SMQ categories, underscoring its clinical relevance for clinicians managing non‑infectious brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1962
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin and dexamethasone acetate are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for the management of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). Fenylefrin may be employed to treat septic shock, while dexamethasone acetate is also considered a possible treatment option for this severe systemic infection. Their use reflects an approach to address the hemodynamic instability and inflammatory response characteristic of septic shock, highlighting the relevance of these drugs in critical care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate, a synthetic glucocorticoid preparation, may be employed in the treatment of shock, where its rapid anti‑inflammatory and vasopressor effects can help stabilize hemodynamics and support organ perfusion in critically ill patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2358
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the therapeutic management of patients who are in a vegetative state (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة), indicating its potential role in addressing the complex medical needs of individuals with severe impairment of consciousness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 260

These overlapping symptom patterns underscore the importance of comprehensive assessment across depressive, encephalitic, and delirious presentations to inform accurate diagnosis and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879

In addition to its inclusion in these complex mixtures, adrenaline is recognized as a treatment for septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة), reflecting its critical role in acute management of severe infection‑induced circulatory failure. The convergence of adrenaline’s pharmacologic activity in both multi‑ingredient oral preparations and emergency therapy underscores its importance in supporting cardiovascular stability and systemic physiological responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 220
retrieved_docs.content: encephalitis spectrum includes the two awakening disorders and is linked to motor and muscular hyperactivity and excessive daytime sleepiness. Likewise, “اللامبالاة” is a component of the delirium/brain‑damage category “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ),” which also comprises attention deficits, reduced concentration, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Collectively, these interrelated entities depict a heterogeneous network in which depressive, psychotic, substance‑use, encephalitic, and delirium‑related conditions share overlapping clinical features—such as reduced affect, hyperactivity, sleep‑wake disturbances, and cognitive impairments—underscoring the necessity for integrated assessment and personalized management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: اللامبالاة (apathy) is identified as a clinical feature of الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome). The syndrome itself is classified under خلل في الانتباه (attention disorder) and under ضعف التركيز (concentration impairment). Thus, attention deficits and reduced concentration are core aspects of delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome, and apathy may appear as one of its manifestations. Recognizing these interrelated signs supports more accurate assessment and management of patients with cognitive and attentional disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 51
retrieved_docs.content: these hierarchical classifications, pharmacodynamic properties, and contraindications is essential for safe prescribing, prevention of harmful drug interactions, and effective management of psychiatric and withdrawal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2133
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin, a potent α‑adrenergic agonist commonly used as a vasopressor, may be employed in the treatment of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). By inducing peripheral vasoconstriction, fenylefrin can help raise systemic vascular resistance and improve arterial blood pressure in patients whose circulatory collapse is driven by overwhelming infection. Its use in septic shock is typically considered when rapid restoration of hemodynamic stability is required, often in conjunction with fluid resuscitation and other supportive measures. Proper dosing and monitoring are essential to balance the benefits of increased perfusion pressure against the risk of excessive vasoconstriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2

Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Overall, the model emphasizes the overlap between organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) and manic patterns such as schizophrenic mania (هوس شَبَقِيّ), and highlights how attention deficits, concentration weakness, and daytime hypersomnolence serve as central elements of non‑infectious delirium, guiding clinicians toward precise behavioral and functional assessment and appropriate therapeutic interventions.
